title: The Sunday Pancake Tradition
summary: A Stockholm narrator describes how her family's slow Sunday pancake breakfast became the most important hour of her week.
body: Every Sunday morning, my family makes pancakes together. It started a few years ago, when my daughter was about six. She wanted to help in the kitchen, so I gave her a small wooden spoon and let her mix the batter. Now she is nine, and she does almost everything by herself. We never eat pancakes during the week, only on Sundays. That is part of the rule. My husband is usually the one who fries them, because he likes them very thin and a little crisp at the edges. I make the coffee and set the table. We always sit in the same places, near the kitchen window. The radio plays quiet music. Sometimes we don't even talk for ten minutes; we just eat and look out at the snow or the rain. After breakfast, we leave the dishes for later. We have a short walk along the river, even when it's cold. My daughter once asked me why we make this so important. I told her that small habits become the days we remember. Now she repeats the same thing to her friends, as if it were her own idea.

annotations.exampleEn: We make pancakes together every Sunday.
Stir the soup with a wooden spoon.
Mix the batter until it is smooth.
She made the cake by herself.
Going to bed at nine is part of the rule.
He fries them in a hot pan with butter.
The biscuits were thin and a little crisp.
Please set the table before dinner.
She looked out at the garden from her window.
We walked slowly along the river.

grammarNotes.title: Present simple for routines and habits
grammarNotes.bodyVi: D√πng th√¨ hi·ªán t·∫°i ƒë∆°n ƒë·ªÉ n√≥i v·ªÅ th√≥i quen ho·∫∑c vi·ªác l·∫∑p ƒëi l·∫∑p l·∫°i. Trong b√Ýi: 'Every Sunday morning, my family makes pancakes together' / 'We always sit in the same places.' Tr·∫°ng t·ª´ ch·ªâ t·∫ßn su·∫•t (always, never, sometimes) th∆∞·ªùng ƒë·ª©ng tr∆∞·ªõc ƒë·ªông t·ª´ th∆∞·ªùng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Present Simple for routines and repeated actions. From the text: 'Every Sunday morning, my family makes pancakes together' and 'We always sit in the same places.' Adverbs of frequency (always, never, sometimes) usually go before the main verb.

grammarNotes.title: Comparatives with 'a little' and 'very'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: D√πng 'a little + adjective' (h∆°i‚Ä¶) ho·∫∑c 'very + adjective' (r·∫•t‚Ä¶) ƒë·ªÉ ƒëi·ªÅu ch·ªânh c∆∞·ªùng ƒë·ªô. Trong b√Ýi: 'a little crisp at the edges' (h∆°i gi√≤n ·ªü m√©p) v√Ý 'very thin' (r·∫•t m·ªèng).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'a little + adjective' to soften a quality, or 'very + adjective' to strengthen it. From the text: 'a little crisp at the edges' and 'very thin'.

questions.prompt: When does the family make pancakes?
questions.explanation: 'Every Sunday morning, my family makes pancakes together.'
questions.hint: Listen for the day at the very beginning.

options: When the daughter was about six
When the daughter was about ten
questions.explanation: 'It started a few years ago, when my daughter was about six.'
questions.hint: Listen for the daughter's age at the start.

questions.prompt: How old is the daughter now?
questions.hint: Listen for a number directly after the start of the tradition.

questions.prompt: Why do they only eat pancakes on Sundays?
options: Because the husband only cooks on Sundays
It is part of the family rule
Because the shop is closed on weekdays

questions.explanation: 'My husband is usually the one who fries them.'
questions.hint: Listen for the family member who likes them thin.

questions.prompt: How does the husband like the pancakes?
options: Very thin and a little crisp at the edges
questions.explanation: 'he likes them very thin and a little crisp at the edges.'
questions.hint: Listen for two adjectives in one phrase.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator do during the morning?
options: She makes the coffee and sets the table
questions.explanation: 'I make the coffee and set the table.'
questions.hint: Listen for two tasks she does.

questions.prompt: What do they sometimes do for ten minutes during breakfast?
options: Just eat and look out of the window without talking
questions.explanation: 'Sometimes we don't even talk for ten minutes; we just eat and look out at the snow or the rain.'
questions.hint: Listen for a quiet, shared moment.

questions.prompt: What do they do after breakfast?
options: Take a short walk along the river
questions.explanation: 'We have a short walk along the river, even when it's cold.'
questions.hint: Listen for what happens after they eat.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator tell her daughter about small habits?
options: They become the days we remember
questions.explanation: 'small habits become the days we remember.'
questions.hint: Listen for the answer she gives her daughter.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Children should help in the kitchen
Simple, repeated family rituals can become the most meaningful part of life
Weekends are the best time to clean the house
questions.explanation: The whole passage celebrates the small, repeated ritual and its emotional weight.
questions.hint: Look at the lesson the narrator teaches her daughter at the end.

cloze.template: Every Sunday morning, my family {{b1}} pancakes together. It started a few years ago, when my daughter was about six. She wanted to help in the kitchen, so I gave her a small {{b2}} spoon and let her mix the batter. Now she is nine, and she does almost everything by {{b3}}. We never eat pancakes during the week, only on Sundays. That is part of the {{b4}}. My husband is usually the one who fries them, because he likes them very thin and a little {{b5}} at the edges. I make the coffee and {{b6}} the table. We always sit in the same places, near the kitchen {{b7}}. The radio plays quiet music. Sometimes we don't even talk for ten minutes; we just eat and look out at the snow or the rain. After breakfast, we leave the {{b8}} for later. We have a short walk along the river, even when it's cold. My daughter once asked me why we make this so important. I told her that small {{b9}} become the days we {{b10}}. Now she repeats the same thing to her friends, as if it were her own idea.

blanks.explanation: 'makes' ‚Äî present simple, third person, fits 'my family'.
'wooden' ‚Äî the spoon was made of wood.
'rule' ‚Äî a family rule about eating pancakes only on Sundays.
'crisp' ‚Äî slightly crunchy, used about cooked food.
'set' ‚Äî 'set the table' means to put plates and cutlery in place.
'window' ‚Äî they sit near the kitchen window.
'dishes' ‚Äî the plates and bowls used during breakfast.
'remember' ‚Äî the days we remember in the future.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says 'small habits become the days we remember.' Think of a small, simple habit in your own family or with a friend. Why do you think the smallest moments often become the strongest memories, more than big events?
